stories.title: Scheffler Three Consecutive Runner-Ups — First Since Garcia in 2014, PGA Championship Now the Target

x_posts.label: Post 1
x_posts.text: Scheffler finished runner-up 3 straight times — Masters, Heritage, Cadillac. First since Garcia (2014) to do it in consecutive starts. He's playing well. Just keeps running into peak rivals. PGA Championship is next. #GolfFanrecap #Golf #Scheffler

facebook.long_form:
Scottie Scheffler is the best golfer in the world right now. His recent results say so. So does his world ranking. And yet he can't seem to win a tournament.

At the Masters, he lost to Rory McIlroy by one shot. At the RBC Heritage, he lost to Matt Fitzpatrick in a playoff. At the Cadillac Championship, he lost to Cameron Young by six strokes. Three consecutive runner-up finishes — the first player since Sergio Garcia in 2014 to finish in that position three starts in a row.

None of this suggests anything is wrong with Scheffler's game. He played well enough to win in all three events. He simply ran into a McIlroy playing at a Masters-peak level, a Fitzpatrick who was locked in at Harbour Town, and a Cameron Young who was practically untouchable across four days in Doral.

In a quieter stretch, Scheffler's finishes would generate headlines for his brilliance. Instead, in the midst of the most competitive stretch of the PGA Tour season, they have generated a pattern that has become its own story.

The good news for Scheffler — and the drama for the rest of the field — is that the next major is right around the corner. He will take a week off before returning to defend his PGA Championship title at Aronimink Golf Club in Newtown Square, Pennsylvania, May 14-17. He has now surpassed $110 million in career PGA Tour earnings. He is world No. 1. He has wins at golf's biggest events. A fourth victory is coming. It feels like it may come soon.
facebook.image_caption: Three straight runner-ups. Three different events. One common result. Scheffler is world No. 1 — and he's channeling everything into defending the PGA Championship at Aronimink in 9 days.

article.headline: Three Straight Runner-Ups: Scheffler's Remarkable Rut and Why the PGA Championship Awaits

stories.title: McIlroy Returns at Quail Hollow — Back-to-Back Masters Champ Eyes Record 5th Truist Title

x_posts.label: Post 2
x_posts.text: Rory McIlroy is back this week at the Truist Championship. First start since winning back-to-back Masters (April 12). He's already won Quail Hollow FOUR times (2010, 2015, 2021, 2024) — he's going for a record 5th title at the same event. #GolfFanrecap #Golf #McIlroy

facebook.long_form:
Rory McIlroy's return to competitive golf this week comes with a certain kind of weight.

On April 12, McIlroy claimed his second consecutive Masters title at Augusta National, joining Jack Nicklaus, Nick Faldo and Tiger Woods as the only men to win back-to-back green jackets. He now holds six major championships — tied with Lee Trevino, Faldo and Phil Mickelson for 12th on the all-time list.

He has not competed since.

His first start since that historic day is at Quail Hollow Club in Charlotte, North Carolina, for the Truist Championship (May 6-10). And if history is any guide, this is as comfortable a venue as he could have chosen for a return. McIlroy has won this event four times: in 2010, 2015, 2021 and 2024. He is seeking a record fifth victory at a single non-major PGA Tour venue.

The field is strong. Cameron Young arrives after his wire-to-wire Cadillac Championship win, having ascended to World No. 3. Matt Fitzpatrick, who has two wins this season himself, is expected. Seven of the top-10 players in the world are in the field.

But none of them arrive carrying what McIlroy carries: two green jackets and the momentum of back-to-back wins at the most storied major in golf. If there is a favorite at Quail Hollow this week, it is him — and it is by a considerable margin.
facebook.image_caption: McIlroy back at Quail Hollow. Two straight Masters wins. Six majors. Four Truist titles. A fifth record win is his target this week.

article.headline: The King Returns: McIlroy Back at Quail Hollow After Back-to-Back Masters, Eyes Record 5th Truist Title

stories.title: Cameron Young Reaches World No. 3 — A Year Ago He Was Outside the Top 60

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text: Cameron Young will move to World No. 3 after his Cadillac Championship win. A year ago he was outside the top 60. In 2026: 2 wins in 4 starts (The Players + Cadillac), beating Scheffler by 6 shots wire-to-wire in his last event. American golf has a new force. #GolfFanrecap #Golf

facebook.long_form:
Twelve months ago, Cameron Young was ranked somewhere outside the top 60 golfers in the world. He was a promising player, one of the longer hitters on tour, someone people had been watching for potential. Not a top-10 star. Not a household name.

Then came 2026.

In March, Young won The Players Championship — the event many consider the "fifth major" — in one of the more dramatic finishes of the year. In May, he won the Cadillac Championship at Trump National Doral, leading all four rounds, shooting 19-under 269 and beating World No. 1 Scottie Scheffler by six shots.

The result will move him to World No. 3 — a career best, and a jump that underscores just how completely this calendar year has transformed his standing in the game.

He now stands among golf's elite. Only Matt Fitzpatrick and Chris Gotterup share with him the distinction of multiple wins in the 2026 PGA Tour season. His combined prize money from the two victories exceeds $6.6 million. He is 27 years old and appears to be entering his prime.

The next tests are immediate. Young is in the field this week at the Truist Championship at Quail Hollow, where he will compete against McIlroy, Scheffler and much of the world's top-10. The PGA Championship at Aronimink follows two weeks later. For a player who was outside the top 60 a year ago, these aren't peripheral events — they're the stage he has now earned a place on.
facebook.image_caption: A year ago: outside the world's top 60. Today: World No. 3. Cameron Young's 2026 season (2 wins in 4 starts) is one of the great breakout stories in recent PGA Tour history.

article.headline: From Outside the Top 60 to World No. 3: Cameron Young's 2026 Season Is Already Remarkable

stories.title: Nelly Korda's 2026 LPGA Season May Be One for the History Books

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text: Nelly Korda: 3 wins and 3 runner-ups in 6 LPGA starts in 2026. SIX events. SIX top-2 finishes. That level of consistency is historically rare — and she's only 5 Hall of Fame points away from LPGA HOF eligibility. #GolfFanrecap #Golf #LPGA #KordaDominance

facebook.long_form:
Six starts. Six top-two finishes. Three wins and three runner-ups.

That is Nelly Korda's record through the first six events of the 2026 LPGA Tour season — and it stands as one of the most dominant early-season stretches in the recent history of women's professional golf.

Her three wins include the AIG Women's Open, the Chevron Championship (her third major title), and last weekend's Riviera Maya Open — a four-shot victory at 17-under in Mexico that was never seriously in doubt from the closing stretch of the final round.

Her three runner-up finishes have come against equally elite competition. Hyo Joo Kim, who has had a remarkable season herself, denied Korda twice at the top of leaderboards. The losses in this context are not failures — they are what happens when two elite players compete at the same events.

What makes Korda's consistency remarkable is not just the wins but the floor. She has not finished lower than second in any event she has entered in 2026. For a sport known for its volatility — where conditions, courses, and random breaks can derail even the best players — maintaining a top-two floor across six tournaments is a statistical rarity.

At 22 of the 27 required Hall of Fame points, Korda is five away from LPGA Hall of Fame eligibility. At the pace she is accumulating points, that threshold may be reached before the year is out. She is 26 years old. The trajectory suggests the best of her career may still be ahead.
facebook.image_caption: Nelly Korda: 3 wins, 3 runner-ups in 6 LPGA starts in 2026. Six events. Six top-2 finishes. She's 5 Hall of Fame points away from LPGA HOF eligibility. This is a historically dominant run.

article.headline: Six Starts, Six Top-Two Finishes: Nelly Korda's 2026 LPGA Season Is Historically Dominant

stories.title: Rahm vs DeChambeau — LIV Golf's Title Race Arrives in Virginia

x_posts.label: Post 5-1
x_posts.text: Rahm: 2 LIV wins in 2026. DeChambeau: 2 LIV wins in 2026. They're co-leading the individual standings heading into LIV Golf Virginia (May 7-10), where $30M is on the line at Trump National. The rivalry that's defining LIV's 2026 season is about to heat up. #GolfFanrecap #LIVGolf

x_posts.label: Post 5-2
x_posts.text: The 2026 golf calendar right now: Truist Championship starts tomorrow. LIV Golf Virginia starts Thursday. PGA Championship at Aronimink in 9 days. Rory vs Young vs Scheffler at Quail Hollow first. What a stretch of golf we have coming. #GolfFanrecap #Golf

facebook.long_form:
If you want to understand the state of LIV Golf in 2026, look at the top of the individual standings: Jon Rahm and Bryson DeChambeau, tied with two wins apiece, heading into LIV Golf Virginia at Trump National Golf Club this Thursday.

It is the 7th event of the 2026 LIV season — roughly the halfway point — and both men arrive having established themselves as the clear forces in the league's title race. Rahm claimed an emphatic six-shot win at LIV Mexico City, closing in a final-round 7-under 64 against a full field. DeChambeau had previously won twice earlier in the season before withdrawing from Mexico City with a wrist injury.

The rivalry between these two is genuine. They have pushed each other across multiple LIV seasons and competed in a playoff earlier in 2026. Both are former major champions (DeChambeau holds the 2020 U.S. Open; Rahm the 2023 U.S. Open and 2021 Masters). Both have re-shaped their games for LIV's format and courses.

Virginia adds another dimension to the storyline. Trump National Golf Club in Sterling, VA — about 45 minutes from Washington, D.C. — offers a setting that tests a complete ball-striking game and rewards precision in the closing holes. The $30 million purse is LIV's standard field-level prize, but the real stake for the leaders is momentum in the individual title race.

Cameron Smith, Tyrrell Hatton, Anthony Kim and the rest of LIV's 57-player field round out a competitive event that, like most LIV tournaments, will have its identity defined by whether Rahm or DeChambeau emerges first.
facebook.image_caption: Rahm: 2 LIV wins in 2026. DeChambeau: 2 LIV wins in 2026. They're tied at the top. LIV Golf Virginia starts Thursday — $30M, Trump National, and a title race heating up.

article.headline: The LIV Title Race Arrives in Virginia: Rahm vs DeChambeau Head Into Thursday as Co-Leaders
